name: Eggplant Parmesan

ingredients.item: 16 oz can San Marzano tomatoes plus one can of water

method: Salt eggplant to take the bitterness out by peeling and slicing the eggplant and placing it in a colander. Sprinkle 1 T salt on the slices and mix, then let sit in the drain for an hour. Rinse off the eggplant well and pat dry. Set aside. In a large frying pan on medium heat, saute onion in 2 T olive oil until lightly browned. Add tomatoes and water. Stir well mashing the tomatoes a little. Add basil and a clove or 2 of crushed garlic. (You can leave the garlic in or take it out later, it’s up to you. I usually take it out.) Add 1 can of water and seasonings and let simmer on low for 20 minutes. Remember to reserve enough sauce to manticare the pasta in. Beat the eggs in a bowl, in a separate bowl, mix breadcrumbs, cheese, salt, pepper, oregano, and parsley. Dip each eggplant slice in the beaten eggs, then breading mixture and set aside on a plate. In a large frying pan on medium heat, add 2 T olive oil and lightly brown both sides of the breaded eggplant. Add more olive oil if needed. Sometimes I need to wash and dry the frying pan and add new oil about half way through frying because the breadcrumbs can accumulate and burn. Also lower the heat if browning too fast. Set eggplant aside on a plate.

When sauce has simmered, put a ladle of sauce on the bottom of a casserole dish and spread, then add an eggplant layer, mozzarella, parmesan, and sauce. Alternate layers until you have used up all the eggplant. Top with more sauce and sprinkle with parmesan and mozzarella. Bake at 350°F for 40 minutes or until the cheese is melted and bubbly. While the eggplant is still cooking, boil a pot of salted water and add 200 grams of pasta, mix, and manticare in reserved sauce with one or two ladles of pasta water, then turn onto plate, place the eggplant on the top or to the side, garnish with grated parmesan and a couple basil leaves. Serves 4. Serving suggestion fresh bread and salad. Olé.
